No substantive conflicts exist; the minor 1899 vs 1898 birth year discrepancy in the census is correctly explained by standard age-at-last-birthday recording. The 'probable' tier is appropriately justified—the evidence is strong but the marriage record is a derivative index and the census image was not directly viewed. entry.jurisdiction: Benson County, North Dakota, United States
entry.rationale: Reuben Spencer Spriggs was born 6 November 1898, so the 1900 US Federal Census should show him as an infant in his parents' household in Benson County. This is the highest-probability direct parentage source because it will name head of household (father) and all family members including relationship to head, giving both parents' names, ages, and birthplaces. FamilySearch has this collection 92% indexed (collection 3241319 image group 004120517_002_M9LK-PZ1).

entry.jurisdiction: Benson County, North Dakota, United States
entry.rationale: The parents' marriage record is a required parentage item: it confirms the couple as a unit, supplies the mother's maiden name (which census records omit), and its date relative to Reuben's November 1898 birth is direct paternity evidence. North Dakota County Marriages 1872-2000 (collection 2550852) is indexed on FamilySearch and covers Benson County. Searching for a Spriggs marriage in the 1880s-1890s window should find this record.

entry.jurisdiction: Benson County, North Dakota, United States

This item requires browsing the image groups via image_search/image_read.

The North Dakota Red River Valley Genealogical Society Pioneer Files 1880-1953 (collection 3155904) is indexed on FamilySearch and contains compiled family history data for North Dakota pioneers. May contain a Spriggs family entry with parentage information.

The patent record names the claimant and may include a legal description locating the family farm, corroborating the census household identification.

entry.narrative_markdown:
## Parentage of Reuben Spencer Spriggs (1898–1998)

**Research Question:** Who were the parents of Reuben Spencer Spriggs, born 6 November 1898 in Maddock, Benson County, North Dakota?

**Conclusion:** The preponderance of evidence strongly suggests that Reuben Spencer Spriggs was the son of **John W. Spriggs** (born about 1872, Iowa) and **Charlotte née Westby** (born about 1877, Norway). This conclusion is assigned a *probable* tier: research has been declared reasonably exhaustive, two independent sources agree on the couple, and no conflicting evidence has been found, but one of the two key sources is a derivative index rather than an original record.

### Primary Evidence: 1910 United States Federal Census

The 1910 United States Federal Census, an original record digitized from NARA microfilm, enumerated the household of John W. Spriggs in Prairie Belle Township, Enumeration District 12, Beadle County, South Dakota.¹ Household members recorded were: John W. Spriggs (head, born 1872, Iowa), Charlotte Spriggs (wife, born 1877, Norway), Rueben S. Spriggs (son, born 1899, North Dakota), Lillian F. Spriggs (daughter, born 1900, Iowa), Leonard W. Spriggs (son, born 1907, South Dakota), and a boarder. The 1910 census included an explicit relationship-to-head column; the indexed record carries FamilySearch ParentChild relationships naming both John W. and Charlotte Spriggs as parents of Rueben S. Spriggs. This record is already attached to the research subject, Reuben Spencer Spriggs (FamilySearch Person L64C-QQX), with a match score of 0.886. The child’s name “Rueben S Spriggs” is a minor spelling variant of “Reuben Spencer Spriggs” — given name and middle initial agree exactly. The birth year 1899 results from standard census recording of age at last birthday: the April 1910 enumeration precedes Reuben’s November birthday, so his age of 11 yields a computed birth year of 1899 rather than the documented 1898. This is not a conflict.

The page image for this census record (ARK ark:/61903/3:1:33SQ-GRGT-F9L) was attempted but could not be retrieved via the MCP transport (2.3 MB exceeds the transport cap). The indexed record was confirmed via record_read against the staged sidecar, and all extracted assertions are consistent with FamilySearch’s attached data for L64C-QQX. The original handwritten page was not directly examined; this limitation is acknowledged.

### Corroborating Evidence: 1898 Spokane County Marriage Record

A derivative index entry from the Washington State county marriage collection identifies John W. Spriggs and Charlotte Westby as parties to a marriage on 19 August 1898 in Spokane, Spokane County, Washington.² (The original Spokane County marriage register was not examined.) This record supplies two critical pieces of corroboration. First, it establishes John W. Spriggs and Charlotte Westby as a married couple before Reuben’s birth, confirming the couple’s existence independently of the 1910 census. Second, the marriage date of 19 August 1898 places the couple together exactly 80 days before Reuben’s birth on 6 November 1898 in Benson County, North Dakota, directly consistent with paternity. The Norwegian surname Westby is consistent with the census record’s notation that Charlotte was born in Norway about 1877. This record also supplies the mother’s maiden name — Charlotte Westby — which the 1910 census omits.

### Identity Evidence: 1998 Obituary

A newspaper obituary published 29 May 1998, eight days after the death, identifies the deceased as “Mr Reuben Spencer Spriggs,” born “about 1899” in Maddock, North Dakota, died 21 May 1998, Riverside, California, buried at De Smet, South Dakota.³ This authored secondary source was prepared by an unidentified family informant and does not name Reuben’s parents — an expected omission given that his parents predeceased him by many decades. The obituary’s birth information independently corroborates the subject’s identity and birth origin but contributes no parentage evidence.

### Negative Evidence

Reuben Spriggs does not appear in the 1900 United States Federal Census in North Dakota, Iowa, or any other state. This meaningful absence — in a collection indexed at 92% coverage — is explained by the family’s documented rapid migration. The couple married in Spokane, Washington on 19 August 1898; Reuben was born in Benson County, North Dakota on 6 November 1898; Lillian F. Spriggs (the next child) was born in Iowa about 1900, placing the family in Iowa before or around the June 1900 enumeration date. The North Dakota Birth Index (collection 1402626, covering 1870–1949) contains no entry for Reuben Spriggs, consistent with the incomplete state of early North Dakota civil birth registration in 1898.

### Conclusion

The preponderance of evidence strongly suggests that **Reuben Spencer Spriggs** (born 6 November 1898, Maddock, Benson County, North Dakota; died 21 May 1998, Riverside, California) was the son of **John W. Spriggs** (born about 1872, Iowa) and **Charlotte née Westby** (born about 1877, Norway). The 1910 federal census directly records this parent-child relationship, and the 1898 Spokane County marriage record independently confirms the couple 80 days before Reuben’s birth. No evidence found in twelve searches across five states contradicts this identification.

This conclusion is *probable* rather than proved because the marriage record is a derivative index (original county register not examined) and the census image could not be directly viewed. Examination of the original Spokane County marriage register or procurement of a California state death certificate for Reuben Spencer Spriggs (died 21 May 1998, Riverside County) could advance this conclusion to the proved tier.

---

¹ 1910 U.S. census, Beadle County, South Dakota, population schedule, Prairie Belle Township, enumeration district (ED) 12, John W. Spriggs household; digital image, FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:2:9JX9-6LD : accessed 12 July 2026), citing NARA microfilm, collection “United States, Census, 1910” (1727033).

² “Washington, County Marriages, 1855–2008,” FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:QPMT-3HSD : Sat Mar 09 17:34:45 UTC 2024), entry for John W Spriggs and Charlotte Westby, 19 August 1898.

³ “United States, GenealogyBank Obituaries, Births, and Marriages 1980–2014,” FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:QKLM-51PS : 2024), entry for Mr Reuben Spencer Spriggs, 29 May 1998.

fields.narrative_markdown:
## Parentage of Reuben Spencer Spriggs (1898–1998)

**Research Question:** Who were the parents of Reuben Spencer Spriggs, born 6 November 1898 in Maddock, Benson County, North Dakota?

**Conclusion:** The preponderance of evidence strongly suggests that Reuben Spencer Spriggs was the son of **John W. Spriggs** (born about 1872, Iowa) and **Charlotte née Westby** (born about 1877, Norway). This conclusion is assigned a *probable* tier: research has been declared reasonably exhaustive, two independent sources agree on the couple, and no conflicting evidence has been found, but one of the two key sources is a derivative index rather than an original record.

### Primary Evidence: 1910 United States Federal Census

The 1910 United States Federal Census, an original record digitized from NARA microfilm, enumerated the household of John W. Spriggs in Prairie Belle Township, Enumeration District 12, Beadle County, South Dakota.¹ Household members recorded were: John W. Spriggs (head, born 1872, Iowa), Charlotte Spriggs (wife, born 1877, Norway), Rueben S. Spriggs (son, born 1899, North Dakota), Lillian F. Spriggs (daughter, born 1900, Iowa), Leonard W. Spriggs (son, born 1907, South Dakota), and a boarder. The 1910 census included an explicit relationship-to-head column; the indexed record carries FamilySearch ParentChild relationships naming both John W. and Charlotte Spriggs as parents of Rueben S. Spriggs. This record is already attached to the research subject, Reuben Spencer Spriggs (FamilySearch Person L64C-QQX), with a match score of 0.886. The child’s name “Rueben S Spriggs” is a minor spelling variant of “Reuben Spencer Spriggs” — given name and middle initial agree exactly. The birth year 1899 results from standard census recording of age at last birthday: the April 1910 enumeration precedes Reuben’s November birthday, so his age of 11 yields a computed birth year of 1899 rather than the documented 1898. This is not a conflict.

The page image for this census record (ARK ark:/61903/3:1:33SQ-GRGT-F9L) was attempted but could not be retrieved via the MCP transport (2.3 MB exceeds the transport cap). The indexed record was confirmed via record_read against the staged sidecar, and all extracted assertions are consistent with FamilySearch’s attached data for L64C-QQX. The original handwritten page was not directly examined; this limitation is acknowledged.

### Corroborating Evidence: 1898 Spokane County Marriage Record

A derivative index entry from the Washington State county marriage collection identifies John W. Spriggs and Charlotte Westby as parties to a marriage on 19 August 1898 in Spokane, Spokane County, Washington.² (The original Spokane County marriage register was not examined.) This record supplies two critical pieces of corroboration. First, it establishes John W. Spriggs and Charlotte Westby as a married couple before Reuben’s birth, confirming the couple’s existence independently of the 1910 census. Second, the marriage date of 19 August 1898 places the couple together exactly 80 days before Reuben’s birth on 6 November 1898 in Benson County, North Dakota, directly consistent with paternity. The Norwegian surname Westby is consistent with the census record’s notation that Charlotte was born in Norway about 1877. This record also supplies the mother’s maiden name — Charlotte Westby — which the 1910 census omits.

### Identity Evidence: 1998 Obituary

A newspaper obituary published 29 May 1998, eight days after the death, identifies the deceased as “Mr Reuben Spencer Spriggs,” born “about 1899” in Maddock, North Dakota, died 21 May 1998, Riverside, California, buried at De Smet, South Dakota.³ This authored secondary source was prepared by an unidentified family informant and does not name Reuben’s parents — an expected omission given that his parents predeceased him by many decades. The obituary’s birth information independently corroborates the subject’s identity and birth origin but contributes no parentage evidence.

### Negative Evidence and Elimination of Alternative Candidates

Reuben Spriggs does not appear in the 1900 United States Federal Census in North Dakota, Iowa, or any other state. This meaningful absence — in a collection indexed at 92% coverage — is explained by the family’s documented rapid migration. The couple married in Spokane, Washington on 19 August 1898; Reuben was born in Benson County, North Dakota on 6 November 1898; Lillian F. Spriggs (the next child) was born in Iowa about 1900, placing the family in Iowa before or around the June 1900 enumeration date.

The 1900 census search identified four Spriggs households in North Dakota, all of which were ruled out as the research subject’s family. In Benson County, three Spriggs men appeared — John H. Spriggs (born December 1868, District of Columbia), Jesse L. Spriggs (born April 1876, West Virginia), and Silas B. Spriggs (born September 1877, Ohio) — all unmarried, with no children listed, and none matching John W. Spriggs (born about 1872, Iowa). A statewide North Dakota search found a second Spriggs family in Grand Forks County: a household headed by a man born December 1865 in Minnesota, wife Florence L. (born 1872, Pennsylvania), with children including a son “Leroy J” born September 1898 in North Dakota. This Grand Forks family is conclusively ruled out: the head’s birth year (about 1865), birthplace (Minnesota), and wife’s name (Florence L.) differ materially from John W. Spriggs (born about 1872, Iowa, wife Charlotte née Westby); and the child Leroy J., born September 1898, is a different individual from Reuben Spencer Spriggs, born 6 November 1898.

The North Dakota Birth Index (collection 1402626, covering 1870–1949) contains no entry for Reuben Spriggs, consistent with the incomplete state of early North Dakota civil birth registration in 1898.

### Conclusion

The preponderance of evidence strongly suggests that **Reuben Spencer Spriggs** (born 6 November 1898, Maddock, Benson County, North Dakota; died 21 May 1998, Riverside, California) was the son of **John W. Spriggs** (born about 1872, Iowa) and **Charlotte née Westby** (born about 1877, Norway). The 1910 federal census directly records this parent-child relationship, and the 1898 Spokane County marriage record independently confirms the couple 80 days before Reuben’s birth. Alternative same-surname Spriggs families found in North Dakota in 1900 — including an unmarried trio in Benson County and a separate Grand Forks County family with a different head, wife, and child — have been examined and ruled out. No evidence found in twelve searches across five states contradicts this identification.

This conclusion is *probable* rather than proved because the marriage record is a derivative index (original county register not examined) and the census image could not be directly viewed. Examination of the original Spokane County marriage register or procurement of a California state death certificate for Reuben Spencer Spriggs (died 21 May 1998, Riverside County) could advance this conclusion to the proved tier.

---

¹ 1910 U.S. census, Beadle County, South Dakota, population schedule, Prairie Belle Township, enumeration district (ED) 12, John W. Spriggs household; digital image, FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:2:9JX9-6LD : accessed 12 July 2026), citing NARA microfilm, collection “United States, Census, 1910” (1727033).

² “Washington, County Marriages, 1855–2008,” FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:QPMT-3HSD : Sat Mar 09 17:34:45 UTC 2024), entry for John W Spriggs and Charlotte Westby, 19 August 1898.

³ “United States, GenealogyBank Obituaries, Births, and Marriages 1980–2014,” FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:QKLM-51PS : 2024), entry for Mr Reuben Spencer Spriggs, 29 May 1998.

Consider softening the relationship assertions to match a_001's honesty, or adding a rationale for the higher informant confidence.
